activities.title: Rainbow's Big Stretch
activities.description: Sit together and tell this story with your whole body: "Once there was a big, bright rainbow that loved to stretch and move. The red part stretched up high like a tall giraffe. The yellow part wiggled side to side like a silly snake. The blue part bent down low like a little cat. The purple part spun round and round like a spinning top." As you tell it, have Sienna stretch, wiggle, bend, and spin with you for each color. After the story, ask: "Which color was your favorite to move like?"
activities.learning: color recognition and body awareness through movement
activities.tip: Ask her to make up a new movement for another color, like orange or green, and copy what she does.

activities.title: Color Letter Hunt
activities.description: Say: "Let's find letters hiding in our colors!" Point to something red and say, "R is for RED — can you say /rrr/?" Then find a yellow object and say, "Y is for YELLOW — can you say /yyy/?" Then a blue object: "B is for BLUE — can you say /bbb/?" Have her point to each object as you make the sound together. Keep it quick and playful — no pressure to remember, just fun sounds.
activities.tip: After each sound, ask her to find another thing in the house that color and clap together while saying the sound.

activities.title: Color Collector Game
activities.description: Give Sienna a simple challenge: "Can you find 3 red things in this room and bring them here?" Count them together as she places each one: "One, two, three!" Then try another color with a different number: "Now find 2 blue things." Count those too. Keep numbers between 1 and 5 so it stays fun and doable. Celebrate each find with a cheer.
activities.learning: counting and number recognition through play
activities.tip: Switch roles: you find objects and let her count them and tell you how many.

activities.title: Rainbow Scribble Painting
activities.description: Give Sienna one piece of paper and crayons in different colors. Say: "Let's make a rainbow! Make big scribbles with the red crayon, then switch to yellow, then blue — whatever colors you want!" As she scribbles, ask her to name each color she uses. She can make circles, waves, or just wild marks — there's no right way. The joy is in moving and making with each color.
activities.learning: fine motor control, color naming, and creative expression
activities.tip: When she's done, ask: "Can you point to the reddest part? The bluest part?" This reinforces color words in a playful way.

activities.title: Rainbow Rain's Big Jump
activities.description: Sit together and tell this story in a warm, animated voice: "Once there was a raindrop named Rainbow Rain. Rainbow Rain was red, and she loved to jump! She jumped up, up, up into the sky. Then she jumped down, down, down to the ground. One day, Rainbow Rain met her friend, a yellow raindrop named Sunny. Sunny said, 'Let's jump together!' So they jumped in a circle—round and round. As they jumped, they made a big, beautiful rainbow in the sky. Rainbow Rain and Sunny were so happy!" Then ask: "If you were a raindrop, what color would you be, and where would you like to jump?"
activities.tip: Ask her to stand up and jump like Rainbow Rain while you retell the story—she'll move her body as she listens.

activities.title: Color Sound Hunt
activities.description: Play a listening and letter game together. Say: "We're going to find things in our house that start with the sound /r/ for red. Can you find something red? Great! Now let's say the sound together: /r/ /r/ /r/." Point to a red object (a toy, blanket, fruit) and have her repeat the /r/ sound three times while you both clap. Then try the same with /b/ for blue and /y/ for yellow. No pressure to write or name letters—just hearing and saying the sounds while moving together.
activities.tip: After finding each color, ask her to hop or spin while saying the sound—it makes it silly and memorable!

activities.title: Color Dot Game
activities.description: Gather small household objects (buttons, cereal pieces, blocks, pebbles—whatever is safe and colorful). Lay them in a pile and say: "Let's find red things! Can you pick one red thing and put it here?" Count it together: "One!" Then: "Can you find two more red things?" Count: "One, two!" Continue until you've counted up to five or six items of the same color, then switch colors. Keep it playful—she's counting by gathering, not sitting still.
activities.tip: Ask: "Do we have more red things or more blue things?" to introduce comparing amounts.

activities.title: Color Dance Painting
activities.description: Give Sienna one crayon (pick a bright color like purple or orange) and a large piece of paper. Say: "Let's make a color dance! Move your arm in big circles, then make zigzags, then wavy lines—whatever your hand wants to do!" As she draws, narrate gently: "Oh, look at those big circles! Now a wavy line!" If she wants, let her switch crayons and layer colors on top. There's no goal—just the joy of movement, color, and marks on paper.
activities.learning: fine motor control, body awareness, creative expression
activities.tip: Play soft music or hum while she draws, and ask: "Does your purple line feel fast or slow?"

activities.title: Police Officer Pete's Big Day
activities.description: Sit together and tell this short story in a warm, animated voice: 'Police Officer Pete woke up at 7 in the morning. He put on his blue uniform and drove his car to help people in town. He stopped to help a little girl find her lost cat. Then he helped an old man cross the street safely. At the end of the day, Pete was so happy because he had helped 3 people! He went home and had a big dinner.' Pause and ask: 'How many people did Police Officer Pete help today?'
activities.tip: Ask Sienna to hold up 3 fingers or count on her fingers along with you as you retell it.

activities.title: Community Helper Sound Hunt
activities.description: Tell Sienna: 'Let's find sounds that Community Helpers make!' Say the /d/ sound and ask her to find things that start with that sound in your home (door, dog, doll). Then try /f/ (fire, fan, fork). When she finds something, say the word together and clap each syllable. Keep it playful—you're listening for sounds, not reading.
activities.tip: Ask: 'Can you make the /f/ sound like a fire truck siren?' and let her have fun with it.

activities.title: Firefighter's Rescue Count
activities.description: Gather 10 small household objects (blocks, toy cars, socks, spoons—anything safe). Tell Sienna: 'The firefighters are rescuing toy animals! Let's line up our toys.' Take turns placing one object at a time, and count together each time: 'One! Two! Three!' Go up to 10, then play again and count backwards. Make it dramatic—each object is a 'rescued' pet or toy.
activities.tip: Ask: 'Can you point to number 5? How many more do we need to reach 10?'

activities.title: Draw a Helper's Helper Tool
activities.description: Give Sienna a piece of paper and one crayon. Say: 'Let's draw something that helps Community Helpers do their job! A doctor uses a stethoscope, or a chef uses a big spoon, or a gardener uses a shovel.' Let her scribble, draw lines, or create her own version. No right or wrong—it's about the fun of creating together.
activities.learning: fine motor skills and imaginative play
activities.tip: Ask: 'What does your helper use to do their job?' and let her tell you a story about her drawing.

activities.title: Shelly the Shy Seahorse
activities.description: Sit together and tell this story in a warm, animated voice: 'Once there was a little seahorse named Shelly who lived in the ocean. Shelly had a friend named Fred the Fish. One day, Shelly said, "S-s-s, I'm scared!" But Fred said, "Don't worry! We have three coral friends who want to play with us!" They swam and swam until they found a big, beautiful reef. Shelly felt so happy! She made the "S" sound again: "S-s-s, so fun!" Would you like to be Shelly the seahorse and make that S sound with me?'
activities.tip: Ask: 'Can you make the S sound like Shelly? What other ocean animals can you think of that start with S?'

activities.title: Ocean Letter Hunt
activities.description: Tell Sienna: 'We're going on a treasure hunt for ocean letters!' Point to objects around your home and say the letter sound together (especially F, S, O, W for Fish, Seahorse, Ocean, Waves). When you find something that starts with that sound—a fork, a sock, an orange—celebrate with a little dance. Keep it light and playful; you're building letter-sound connection, not perfection.
activities.learning: Letter sound recognition and phonemic awareness
activities.tip: Play a listening game: 'I spy with my little eye something that starts with /f/!' and let her guess around the room.

activities.title: Counting Starfish
activities.description: Gather 5–8 small household objects (buttons, crackers, blocks, or even socks) and lay them in front of Sienna. Say: 'These are magic starfish hiding in the ocean! Let's count how many we have.' Point to each one together and count aloud. Then hide one starfish under your hand and ask: 'How many starfish do we see now?' Repeat, adding and removing starfish as she watches.
activities.tip: Ask: 'If we add one more starfish, how many will we have?' Let her experiment and count again together.

activities.title: Wavy Ocean Picture
activities.description: Give Sienna a piece of paper and a blue or green crayon. Say: 'Let's draw the ocean! Make wavy lines for the water—like this,' and show her a gentle wavy motion. Then say, 'Now you try! Make your own waves.' Once she's made some waves, draw or let her draw simple circles or blobs for fish, bubbles, or starfish. Keep it loose and joyful—there's no 'right way.'
activities.learning: Fine motor control and creative expression
activities.tip: As she draws, name the colors and shapes: 'Oh, you made a blue wave! Can you make a big circle for a jellyfish?'
